event: Vision of Christ enthroned with saints
symbols: Sacred Heart on Christ's chest — the burning love of God for humanity, the devotion to Christ's redeeming love
outstretched arms of Christ — universal embrace of redemption, echoing the posture of the Crucifixion
crown of thorns as halo — Christ's suffering transformed into glory
angels flanking the throne — the heavenly court attending the King of Kings
radiant light emanating from Christ — divine glory, the uncreated light of the Godhead
description: A large-scale composition centered on a standing figure of Christ in a white robe with arms spread wide. A radiant Sacred Heart glows on his chest and rays of light emanate from his entire figure. He wears a crown of thorns. Flanking him are two figures: to the left a figure in dark armor with a halo (Joan of Arc), and to the right a winged angel holding a banner. Smaller angelic figures appear at the base. The background features ornate architectural framing with inscriptions in Latin along the top. The color palette is dominated by gold, blue, and white.
theological_description: This monumental image presents the glorified Christ displaying the Sacred Heart, a devotion rooted in the revelations to Saint Margaret Mary Alacoque but grounded in the scriptural witness to Christ's pierced side (John 19:34). The outstretched arms recall the Crucifixion posture now transformed into a gesture of universal invitation: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden' (Matt 11:28). The crown of thorns remains even in glory, signifying that the wounds of the Passion are eternally present in the Risen Body — as Thomas was invited to touch them (John 20:27). Joan of Arc, the warrior-saint, stands as a type of the Church Militant, while the angel with a banner represents the Church Triumphant. Together they flank the cosmic Christ who reigns over all history. The Latin inscriptions and architectural framing suggest a liturgical context, placing this image within the tradition of apse mosaics and altar decorations that present Christ as Pantocrator — the ruler of all — whose Sacred Heart is the font of all grace and mercy.

scripture_refs.reason: The piercing of Christ's side from which flowed blood and water, the scriptural foundation of Sacred Heart devotion
Christ's invitation with outstretched arms to all who are weary, reflected in the welcoming posture
The glorified Christ of Revelation standing in majesty, the Alpha and Omega
The wounds of Christ visible even in glory, as when Thomas was invited to touch them
The heavenly throne scene with attendant beings, echoed in the angels flanking Christ
